steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Git hooks are failing on /home/user/project with "fatal: not in a git directory" but only when triggered by push — running `git status` or `git log` works fine, and running the hook script manually (`bash .git/hooks/pre-push`) also works. Started after we moved the repo from /srv/repos to /home/user. The hook itself is just a few lines that call `git rev-parse --verify HEAD` and log to /var/log/git-hooks.log. Hooks were working before the move so I'm guessing something path-related but I've already checked GIT_DIR and it's not set anywhere weird.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@591b1362c395:~$

Analysis: Starting fresh in the home directory. The issue is that git hooks fail with 'fatal: not in a git directory' when triggered by push, but work when run manually. This suggests an environment or working directory issue during the push operation. Need to examine the repository structure, hook scripts, and git configuration.
Plan: First, I'll navigate to the project directory and examine its structure. Then I'll check the git configuration, look at the hook script content, and investigate any potential path or environment issues that might occur during push operations.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~$ cd /home/user/project
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ pwd
/home/user/project
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ ls -la
total 52
drwxr-xr-x 1 user user 4096 Apr 29 09:53 .
drwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4096 Apr 29 09:53 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 user user 4096 Apr 29 09:53 .git
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file1.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   22 Apr 29 09:53 file10.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file2.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file3.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file4.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file5.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file6.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file7.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file8.txt
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user   21 Apr 29 09:53 file9.txt
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Successfully navigated to /home/user/project. The directory contains a .git folder and several files. Now I need to examine the pre-push hook script and check the git configuration for any path-related issues.
Plan: I'll check the pre-push hook script content, examine git config for any suspicious settings, and look for any hardcoded paths from the old location (/srv/repos). I'll also check if there are any symbolic links or git configuration that might be causing path issues during push operations.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ cat .git/hooks/pre-push
#!/bin/bash
cd /srv/repos/project
HASH=$(git rev-parse --verify HEAD)
echo "$(date): push verified, HEAD=$HASH" >> /var/log/git-hooks.log
exit 0
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ git config --list --local
core.repositoryformatversion=0
core.filemode=true
core.bare=false
core.logallrefupdates=true
remote.origin.url=/home/user/project.git
remote.origin.fetch=+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Found the problem! The pre-push hook has a hardcoded `cd /srv/repos/project` command on line 2, which is the old repository path. When the hook runs during a push operation, it tries to change to /srv/repos/project (which no longer exists or is not the current repo), causing the 'fatal: not in a git directory' error. When run manually with `bash .git/hooks/pre-push`, it fails to cd but the script continues and git commands might still work from the current directory context, or the error isn't as visible.
Plan: I need to fix the pre-push hook by updating the path from /srv/repos/project to /home/user/project. I'll edit the hook file to correct this path issue.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ sed -i 's|cd /srv/repos/project|cd /home/user/project|' .git/hooks/pre-push
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ cat .git/hooks/pre-push
#!/bin/bash
cd /home/user/project
HASH=$(git rev-parse --verify HEAD)
echo "$(date): push verified, HEAD=$HASH" >> /var/log/git-hooks.log
exit 0
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Successfully updated the pre-push hook to use the correct path (/home/user/project instead of /srv/repos/project). Now I should verify that the hook works correctly by testing it manually and ensuring it can execute git commands properly.
Plan: I'll test the hook by running it manually to confirm it works, and also verify that the log file location is writable. Then I'll do a test push to ensure the hook executes successfully during an actual push operation.

arguments.keystrokes: ls -la /var/log/git-hooks.log 2>&1 || echo 'Log file does not exist yet or not accessible'

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ bash .git/hooks/pre-push
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ echo $?
0
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ ls -la /var/log/git-hooks.log 2>&1 || echo 'Log file does not exist yet or not accessible'
-rw-rw-rw- 1 root root 91 Apr 29 09:54 /var/log/git-hooks.log
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The hook now runs successfully (exit code 0) and the log file exists and is writable. Let me verify the log content to confirm the hook is working properly, and then test with an actual git push operation to ensure it works in the real scenario.
Plan: I'll check the log file content to see if the hook successfully logged the push verification. Then I'll attempt a test push to verify the hook works during an actual push operation.

arguments.keystrokes: git push origin main 2>&1 || git push origin master 2>&1

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ cat /var/log/git-hooks.log
Wed Apr 29 09:54:23 UTC 2026: push verified, HEAD=cc749b8c516da3db208650d627822298c3237006
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ git remote -v
origin  /home/user/project.git (fetch)
origin  /home/user/project.git (push)
user@591b1362c395:~/project$ git push origin main 2>&1 || git push origin master 2>&1
Everything up-to-date
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The hook is now working correctly. The log shows a successful execution with the HEAD hash, and the git push command completed without any 'fatal: not in a git directory' errors. The hook executed during the push operation (even though everything was up-to-date). Let me verify the log file one more time to confirm the hook ran during the push.
Plan: I'll check the log file again to see if a new entry was added during the push operation, confirming the hook is functioning properly in the actual push scenario.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ cat /var/log/git-hooks.log
Wed Apr 29 09:54:23 UTC 2026: push verified, HEAD=cc749b8c516da3db208650d627822298c3237006
Wed Apr 29 09:54:29 UTC 2026: push verified, HEAD=cc749b8c516da3db208650d627822298c3237006
user@591b1362c395:~/project$

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The log file now shows two entries - one from the manual test and one from the actual git push operation (note the different timestamps: 09:54:23 and 09:54:29). This confirms the hook is now working correctly during push operations. The issue has been resolved.
Plan: The task is complete. The problem was that the pre-push hook contained a hardcoded path to the old repository location (/srv/repos/project). After updating it to the new location (/home/user/project), the hook now executes successfully both manually and during actual push operations.

arguments.keystrokes: echo 'Git hook issue resolved! The pre-push hook was using the old path /srv/repos/project instead of /home/user/project.'

results.content:
Current terminal state:
New Terminal Output:

user@591b1362c395:~/project$ echo 'Git hook issue resolved! The pre-push hook was using the old path /srv/repos/project instead of /home/user/project.'
Git hook issue resolved! The pre-push hook was using the old path /srv/repos/project instead of /home/user/project.
user@591b1362c395:~/project$


Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

steps.message:
Analysis: The task has been successfully completed. The root cause was identified: the pre-push hook contained a hardcoded `cd /srv/repos/project` command that pointed to the old repository location. After the repository was moved to /home/user/project, this caused the hook to fail with 'fatal: not in a git directory' during push operations. The fix was simple - update the path in the hook from /srv/repos/project to /home/user/project. Testing confirms the hook now works correctly both when run manually and during actual git push operations, as evidenced by the two log entries with different timestamps.
Plan: The solution is verified and complete. The pre-push hook has been corrected and is functioning properly.
